en.extract:
The black-tailed treecreeper (Climacteris melanurus) is a species of bird in the family Climacteridae. It is endemic to north and northwestern Australia and is the only treecreeper species found throughout most of its range. It belongs to the genus Climacteris and is noticeably darker than the other members of the genus.
Its natural habitats are temperate forests and subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests.


Description and Habitat
The black-tailed treecreeper is small to medium in size, generally measuring between 16–20 centimetres (6.3–7.9 in) in length with a weight of 27–36 grams (0.95–1.27 oz). It has a wingspan of 8.7–10.2 centimetres (3.4–4.0 in) and a white wing-bar that is highly visible during flight. Both sexes are dark brown and black-tailed with large feet. The male has a black throat with white streaking, while the female has a white throat and white streaking through a reddish-brown upper breast. Similar in appearance to the brown treecreeper of eastern Australia, with no white supercilium being the most obvious distinction.
Habitat includes open grassy woodlands, savanna, dry forest, and moist lowland forest. It is the only treecreeper species found in northern Australia, and normally travels in pairs or trios, and occasionally small groups.


Calls
It is most often heard giving a contact call, a loud, metallic chee-ting or ching, often singly or in repeated 1–2 second intervals. When feeding or in flight, a quick burst of softer notes may be heard. Other calls include a slow, repeated pip pip pip.


Breeding
They participate in cooperative breeding, in which multiple individuals assist in raising young — not just the biological parents. A clutch of 1–3 pinkish-white, reddish-brown spotted or purplish-red spotted eggs are laid, measuring around 25 by 19 millimetres (0.98 in × 0.75 in). Nests are made of fur, grass, and feathers, normally in hollowed out tree trunks and branches. Incubation takes between 14–24 days. They likely reach sexual maturity around 2 years old.


Feeding and behavior
The black-tailed treecreeper is an insectivore, primarily feeding on invertebrates found beneath the bark of tree trunks and branches, and occasionally on the ground. They generally land near the base of a tree and climb rapidly upwards, often spiraling around the trunk as they search for food — a common trait among Australasian treecreepers. It is considered sedentary and does not migrate, preferring to stay in the same general area year-round.


Evolution
The black-tailed treecreeper is thought to be the closest living relative (a sister taxon) of the brown treecreeper. The two birds were likely a single species, separated over time by the Carpentarian Barrier — a large, historically sparse and dry area in north-eastern Australia, which prevented the two groups from interacting. They diverged genetically and remain allopatric, but share many similarities, including appearance, habitat and similar mannerisms. It is also closely related to the rufous treecreeper of southern and southwestern Australia.


Taxonomy
Climacteris melanurus was first described as C. melanura by the English ornithologist John Gould in an 1843 publishing of the journal Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London, of which Gould was a member. The description was based on a specimen collected by Benjamin Bynoe near modern-day Derby, during the third voyage of HMS Beagle to Australia. Around the same time, Gould himself had also been in Australia on an unrelated trip, collecting bird specimens for his book The Birds of Australia, in which the species would later be published. The genus name Climacteris is derived from the Greek word klimaktēr (κλιμακτήρ), meaning 'rung of a ladder', referring to the birds' habit of climbing vertically up tree trunks. The specific epithet melanurus derives from the Greek words melas (meaning black) and ourā́ (meaning tail), hence the common name, "black-tailed".


Subspecies
There are two recognised subspecies:

C. m. melanurus – The nominate subspecies, found across Northern Australia from the Kimberley region in Western Australia to the Leichhardt River in Queensland.
C. m. wellsi Ogilvie-Grant, 1909 – Only found in the Pilbara region of northwest Western Australia, between the De Grey and Fortescue Rivers. It was originally described by William Robert Ogilvie-Grant as a separate species, Climacteris wellsi, in 1909, but was later reclassified as a subspecies. Before being reclassed, it was known as the Allied treecreeper. Mitochondrial DNA testing has since confirmed that the two birds are deeply divergent, further supporting their status as subspecies.
The subspecies differ slightly in appearance; C. m. wellsi is slightly smaller and paler, with females having more extensive streaking through the breast, and rufous-brown underparts. Their ranges do not overlap, separated by the Great Sandy Desert of northwestern Australia, in which there have been no sightings.


Conservation
The black-tailed treecreeper is assessed as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List. The population is considered stable, with an extremely large range.


See also
Australasian treecreeper


References

The '''black-tailed treecreeper''' ('''''Climacteris melanurus''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Climacteridae]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to north and northwestern Australia and is the only treecreeper species found throughout most of its range. It belongs to the [[genus]] [[Climacteris]] and is noticeably darker than the other members of the genus.

Its natural [[habitat]]s are temperate [[forest]]s and subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests.<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" />

== Description and Habitat ==
The black-tailed treecreeper is small to medium in size, generally measuring between {{convert|16|–|20|cm}} in length with a weight of {{convert|27|–|36|g}}.<ref name=" abcd " /><ref name= slater >{{cite book |last1=Slater |first1=Peter |last2=Slater |first2=Pat |last3=Slater |first3=Raoul |title=[[The Slater Field Guide to Australian Birds]] (Revised Edition)|pages=270|year=1995 |isbn=0-947116-99-0 |publisher=Lansdowne |location=Sydney, Australia}}</ref> It has a wingspan of {{convert|8.7|–|10.2|cm}} and a white wing-bar that is highly visible during flight. Both sexes are dark brown and black-tailed with large feet. The male has a black throat with white streaking, while the female has a white throat and white streaking through a reddish-brown upper breast.<ref name=morcombe>{{cite book|title=Field guide to Australian birds|first=Michael|last=Morcombe|publisher=Steve Parish Publishing|year=2004}}</ref><ref name=" treecreeper " /> Similar in appearance to the [[brown treecreeper]] of eastern Australia, with no white [[supercilium]] being the most obvious distinction.<ref name=abcd>{{Cite book|title=The Australian Bird Guide (Revised Edition)|last1=Menkhorst |first1=P. |last2=Rogers |first2=D. |last3=Clarke |first3=R. |last4=Davies |first4=J. |last5=Marsack |first5=P. |last6=Franklin |first6=K.|publisher=CSIRO Publications|isbn=978-1-4863-1193-4|pages=422|year=2019|location=Victoria}}</ref>

Habitat includes open grassy woodlands, savanna, dry forest, and moist lowland forest.<ref name=" abc " /> It is the only treecreeper species found in northern Australia, and normally travels in pairs or trios, and occasionally small groups.<ref name=" abcd  " /><ref name=" abc " />

=== Calls ===
It is most often heard giving a contact call, a loud, metallic ''chee-ting'' or ''ching'', often singly or in repeated 1–2 second intervals. When feeding or in flight, a quick burst of softer notes may be heard.<ref name=" abcd  " /> Other calls include a slow, repeated ''pip pip pip''.<ref name=abc>{{Cite web |title=Black-tailed treecreeper - eBird |url=https://ebird.org/species/bkttre1?siteLanguage=en_AU|access-date=2025-12-09|website=ebird.org |language=en}}</ref>

== Breeding ==
They participate in cooperative breeding, in which multiple individuals assist in raising young — not just the biological parents.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Edwards |first1=Scott V |last2=Tonini |first2=João F R |last3=Mcinerney |first3=Nancy |last4=Welch |first4=Corey |last5=Beerli |first5=Peter |date=1 February 2023 |title=Multilocus phylogeography, population genetics and niche evolution of Australian brown and black-tailed treecreepers (Aves:''Climacteris'') |journal=Biological Journal of the Linnean Society |volume=138 |issue=3 |pages=249–273 |doi=10.1093/biolinnean/blac144 |issn=0024-4066|doi-access=free }}</ref> A clutch of 1–3 pinkish-white, reddish-brown spotted or purplish-red spotted eggs are laid, measuring around {{convert|25|x|19|mm}}. Nests are made of fur, grass, and feathers, normally in hollowed out tree trunks and branches.<ref name=" abcd  " /><ref name=" slater " /> Incubation takes between 14–24 days.<ref name=" RVJ " /> They likely reach sexual maturity around 2 years old.

== Feeding and behavior ==
The black-tailed treecreeper is an insectivore, primarily feeding on invertebrates found beneath the bark of tree trunks and branches, and occasionally on the ground.<ref name=" treecreeper  " /> They generally land near the base of a tree and climb rapidly upwards, often spiraling around the trunk as they search for food — a common trait among Australasian treecreepers. It is considered sedentary and does not migrate, preferring to stay in the same general area year-round.<ref name=cd>{{cite web|title=Black-tailed Treecreeper|url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/black-tailed-treecreeper-climacteris-melanurus|work=BirdLife|accessdate=13 January 2026}}</ref>

== Evolution ==
The black-tailed treecreeper is thought to be the closest living relative (a [[Sister group|sister taxon]]) of the [[brown treecreeper]]. The two birds were likely a single species, separated over time by the [[Carpentarian Barrier]] — a large, historically sparse and dry area in north-eastern Australia, which prevented the two groups from interacting. They diverged genetically and remain [[Allopatric speciation|allopatric]], but share many similarities, including appearance, habitat and similar mannerisms. It is also closely related to the [[rufous treecreeper]] of southern and southwestern Australia.<ref name=":0" />
[[File:The birds of Australia (16839364916) (cropped).jpg|thumb|An illustration of the black-tailed treecreeper from Gracius Broinowski's book ''The birds of Australia'']]

== Taxonomy ==
''Climacteris melanurus'' was first described as ''C. melanura'' by the English ornithologist [[John Gould]] in an 1843 publishing of the journal ''[[Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London]]'', of which Gould was a member.<ref>{{Cite journal|author-link=John Gould|last=Gould|first=John|date=1843|title=Descriptions of thirty New Species of Birds from Australia|journal=[[Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London]]|volume=10|issue=10–11|pages=138–139|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30679952#page/290/mode/1up}}</ref> The description was based on a specimen collected by [[Benjamin Bynoe]] near modern-day [[Derby, Western Australia|Derby]], during the third voyage of {{HMS|Beagle}} to Australia.<ref>{{cite book|vauthors= ((Gould,  Elizabeth)), ((Gould,  John)), ((Richter,  Henry Constantine))|date= 1840|title=The birds of Australia|publisher= Printed by R. and J. E. Taylor|volume=4|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48703204}}</ref> Around the same time, Gould himself had also been in Australia on an unrelated trip, collecting bird specimens for his book ''[[The Birds of Australia (Gould)|The Birds of Australia]]'', in which the species would later be published. The genus name ''Climacteris'' is derived from the [[Greek language|Greek]] word ''[[wikt:κλιμακτήρ|klimaktēr]]'' (κλιμακτήρ), meaning 'rung of a ladder', referring to the birds' habit of climbing vertically up tree trunks. The [[Specific name (zoology)|specific epithet]] ''melanurus'' derives from the Greek words ''[[wikt:melas|melas]]'' (meaning black) and ''[[wikt:οὐρά|ourā́]]'' (meaning tail), hence the common name, "black-tailed".<ref>{{cite web|title=Climacteric|url=https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/climacteric|work=[[Merriam-Webster]]|accessdate=3 January 2025}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last=Jobling |first=James A. |title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names |year=2010 |publisher=Christopher Helm |location=London |isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4|pages=112, 248|url=https://www.avesdecostarica.org/uploads/7/0/1/0/70104897/scientific-bird-names.pdf}}</ref>
[[File:Black-tailed tree creeper chick on a tree - DPLA - 0355489e94650ea688cc04f503312ec0.jpg|thumb|A black-tailed treecreeper chick on a tree]]

=== Subspecies ===
There are two recognised subspecies:

* ''C. m. melanurus'' – The nominate subspecies, found across [[Northern Australia]] from the [[Kimberley region]] in [[Western Australia]] to the [[Leichhardt River]] in [[Queensland]].<ref name=" treecreeper ">{{ cite journal |title=Black-tailed Treecreeper |journal=Western Australian Museum Collections|url=https://museum.wa.gov.au/online-collections/names/climacteris-melanura |access-date=10 November 2025}}</ref>

* ''C. m. wellsi'' <small>Ogilvie-Grant, 1909</small> – Only found in the [[Pilbara]] region of northwest Western Australia, between the [[De Grey River|De Grey]] and [[Fortescue River|Fortescue Rivers]]. It was originally described by [[William Robert Ogilvie-Grant]] as a separate species, ''Climacteris wellsi'', in 1909, but was later reclassified as a subspecies.<ref>{{Cite journal|author-link=William Robert Ogilvie-Grant|last=Ogilvie-Grant|first=WR|date=1909|title=On a collection of birds from Western Australia|journal=Ibis|volume=3|issue=4 |pages=664| doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1909.tb05276.x|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/269806#page/764/mode/1up}}</ref> Before being reclassed, it was known as the '''Allied treecreeper'''.<ref>{{cite web|title= Black-tailed Treecreeper / Climacteris melanurus LC|url=https://dibird.com/species/black-tailed-treecreeper/|work=DiBird|accessdate=3 January 2026}}</ref> Mitochondrial DNA testing has since confirmed that the two birds are deeply divergent, further supporting their status as subspecies.<ref name=" treecreeper  " />

The subspecies differ slightly in appearance; ''C. m. wellsi'' is slightly smaller and paler, with females having more extensive streaking through the breast, and rufous-brown underparts.<ref name=" abcd " /> Their ranges do not overlap, separated by the [[Great Sandy Desert]] of northwestern Australia, in which there have been no sightings.<ref name=" treecreeper "/><ref name=" RVJ ">{{cite journal |last1=Noske|first1=R. |editor-last=del Hoyo |editor-first=J. |editor-last2=Elliott |editor-first2=A.|editor-last3=Sargatal |editor-first3=J. |editor-last4=Christie |editor-first4=D. A. |editor-last5=de Juana|editor-first5= E.|date=2020 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/bkttre1/cur/introduction|title=Black-tailed Treecreeper (Climacteris melanurus), version 1.0. In Birds of the World|journal=[[Birds of the World]]|location= Ithaca, NY, USA. |doi=10.2173/bow.bkttre1.01|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

== Conservation ==
The black-tailed treecreeper is assessed as Least Concern on the [[IUCN Red List]]. The population is considered stable, with an extremely large range.<ref name=" cd  " />

== See also ==
* [[Australasian treecreeper]]
